metadata.from: Bob (Robert E. Hanifen) — confirmed by return address on envelope: P.F.C. Robert E. Hanifen 17150600, 2012th Ord. & Mnt. Co. BE APO 528, c/o P.M. New York, N.Y. Signed 'Bobby' at close.
metadata.to: Jim (James E. Hanifen Jr.) — recipient on envelope: Cpl. James E. Hanifen 37693423, 1947 SCU, Camp San Luis Obispo, California. This is Bob's brother Jim Jr., as both are in military service.
metadata.location_written_from: Caserta, Italy (mentioned in letter: 'I am working in the Inspector office here at Caserta')
metadata.location_sent_to: Cpl. James E. Hanifen 37693423, 1947 SCU, Camp San Luis Obispo, California
metadata.postmark: U.S. Army Postal Service, APR [date partially illegible], 512
metadata.envelope_notes: 6-cent Air Mail stamp (red, U.S. airplane design). Return address written in print: P.F.C. Robert E. Hanifen 17150600, 2012th Ord. & Mnt. Co. BE APO 528, c/o P.M. New York, N.Y. Recipient address in cursive. Note: the year in the letter date appears to read '1946' though the postmark year is partially illegible.
topics: inspection tour to Bari and Manfredonia
German soldiers described as swell guys
brother stationed in Los Angeles / transferred to San Luis Obispo
metadata.mood_tone: Lively, humorous, and brotherly — Bob writes with a casual and joking tone, sharing entertaining anecdotes about near-marriage, jeep driving, Italian women, and army life. There is also an undercurrent of wariness regarding theft and safety.
metadata.legibility_notes: Most of the letter is readable with careful attention. Some words are difficult due to cursive flourishes and faded ink, particularly on pages 3 and 4. The word 'fratello' (Italian for brother) is used as a greeting and closing, which is distinctive. Some words required best-guess interpretation. The date year on page 1 could be read as '1946' or conceivably '1940' but context (WWII over, stationed in Caserta, APO) confirms 1946.
metadata.historical_context: Written in the post-WWII occupation period. Bob is stationed at Caserta, Italy, a major Allied headquarters during and after WWII. References to Cassino and Salerno reflect major WWII battle sites in Italy. Mentions of German soldiers as 'swell guys' suggests fraternization during occupation. References to P-38 and Luger German pistols, and guarded barracks/parking lots indicate ongoing security concerns in occupied Italy.

pages.transcription:
April 2, 1946

Dear Fratello Jim

In Italian it means brother. Well Jim I receive your letter and I am very glad to hear of your good fortune. Stationed in L.A. right near home is a pretty nice if you ask me. Larry about your being transferred to San Louis Obispo. Was sorry to hear that the motor fell out of the Chev. rolled — hell did you do. Tels of about 80 miles a hour. I drive the living hell out of my jeep "Mile Butch" is its name. That has a gun really move. It should though it had everything put on it except a new motor. I would really like to see Betty + Morton faces when Jim my boy hulled in. Bet it really floored them.

Bet it really floored them. I told you I am working in the Inspector office here at Caserta. I should be a Corporal before long I hope. Everybody is getting ratings. I am doing the work by myself since the Lt. went to Switzerchland on his vacation. I should get something at least by god I know the reasons why. The girls are very nice here, that is the nice ones. I also had myself married about 2 weeks ago. This girl was mad for my body + so was her mother. The nice girls go everywhere with their mothers. I got out of it just in time. She was a cute bit though, stacked my god was she. I seen Jack Buckley in Foggia a couple weeks ago when we to Bari + Manfredonia

for an Inspection tour. Look on the map it is on the Adriatic sea. These towns were shot to hell. I haven't been to Salerno or Cassino yet. But the boys says they are nothing but rumble as far as that. This town is full of holes. Jack Buckley is getting me a camera. Sweet guy.

My buddies here is getting me to carry a pistol at night by yourself you need something to protect yourself with. Our barracks area and parking lots are always under guards. These Guinies will steal you blind if they get a chance. I am trying to get hold of a P-38 or Luger, German guns. These Lineys over here all have them. They are really a swell gun. a 45 is pretty big to lug around all the time. Send me some pictures of yourself and I will do the

same. Write and tell if your girl would like a few cameo rings or necklaces or what have you. They cheap as hell here. Send me a few cartons of cigarettes and I can get anything. They sell here for 2700 lire a carton. That is about 130 cent a pack in American dough. Haven't gained any weight yet. But I hope I do. Could stand about 30 lbs. I recone you could to. The weather is very nice now. Hot in day time, cold as hell at night. They say it really gets hot here in the summer. These Germans are swell guys. Do anything you ask. They will sell anything if they can possibly do it. These Dieseboys though can [otta?] go to hell. Well.

Jimmy old man goodby for now.

Lots of Love Yours Fratello

Bobby

P.S. Picture of me + my buddie
San Venora. Put it in your album.

full_transcription:
April 2, 1946

Dear Fratello Jim

In Italian it means brother. Well Jim I receive your letter and I am very glad to hear of your good fortune. Stationed in L.A. right near home is a pretty nice if you ask me. Larry about your being transferred to San Louis Obispo. Was sorry to hear that the motor fell out of the Chev. rolled — hell did you do. Tels of about 80 miles a hour. I drive the living hell out of my jeep "Mile Butch" is its name. That has a gun really move. It should though it had everything put on it except a new motor. I would really like to see Betty + Morton faces when Jim my boy hulled in. Bet it really floored them. I told you I am working in the Inspector office here at Caserta. I should be a Corporal before long I hope. Everybody is getting ratings. I am doing the work by myself since the Lt. went to Switzerchland on his vacation. I should get something at least by god I know the reasons why. The girls are very nice here, that is the nice ones. I also had myself married about 2 weeks ago. This girl was mad for my body + so was her mother. The nice girls go everywhere with their mothers. I got out of it just in time. She was a cute bit though, stacked my god was she. I seen Jack Buckley in Foggia a couple weeks ago when we to Bari + Manfredonia for an Inspection tour. Look on the map it is on the Adriatic sea. These towns were shot to hell. I haven't been to Salerno or Cassino yet. But the boys says they are nothing but rumble as far as that. This town is full of holes. Jack Buckley is getting me a camera. Sweet guy.

My buddies here is getting me to carry a pistol at night by yourself you need something to protect yourself with. Our barracks area and parking lots are always under guards. These Guinies will steal you blind if they get a chance. I am trying to get hold of a P-38 or Luger, German guns. These Lineys over here all have them. They are really a swell gun. a 45 is pretty big to lug around all the time. Send me some pictures of yourself and I will do the same. Write and tell if your girl would like a few cameo rings or necklaces or what have you. They cheap as hell here. Send me a few cartons of cigarettes and I can get anything. They sell here for 2700 lire a carton. That is about 130 cent a pack in American dough. Haven't gained any weight yet. But I hope I do. Could stand about 30 lbs. I recone you could to. The weather is very nice now. Hot in day time, cold as hell at night. They say it really gets hot here in the summer. These Germans are swell guys. Do anything you ask. They will sell anything if they can possibly do it. These Dieseboys though can [otta?] go to hell. Well.

Jimmy old man goodby for now.

Lots of Love Yours Fratello

Bobby

P.S. Picture of me + my buddie
San Venora. Put it in your album.
summary: In this April 1946 letter, PFC Bob Hanifen writes from Caserta, Italy to his brother Jim (Cpl. James E. Hanifen Jr.) at Camp San Luis Obispo, California. Bob shares colorful anecdotes from occupied Italy: working in the Inspector's office while his lieutenant vacations in Switzerland, nearly getting entangled in marriage with an Italian girl and her mother, encountering Jack Buckley in Foggia during an inspection tour to Bari and Manfredonia, and navigating post-war conditions including theft risks and seeking a German P-38 or Luger pistol for personal protection. He also makes practical requests — cartons of cigarettes (valuable currency in Italy at 2700 lire per carton) and photos — and offers to send cameo jewelry in return. The letter is a vivid snapshot of American GI life during the post-WWII Italian occupation, written with characteristic Hanifen humor and brotherly affection.
